Explanation of Suspicion: Relies on land-based logistics. While ocean freight is less of a factor, domestic diesel prices hike harvesting and trucking. Production is hit by petroleum-based resin costs.
Faces extreme ocean freight volatility due to bunkering costs and rerouting around conflict zones. Production is affected by imported resins and energy for kilns.
Similar to Vietnam, relies heavily on trans-Pacific shipping. High fuel surcharges and energy-dependent manufacturing drive the price hike.
Long-haul ocean freight is the main driver. Harvesting in vast interiors requires high diesel consumption for machinery and transport to ports. Resins are mostly imported.
Massive inland distances from forest to port mean diesel price spikes are immediately reflected. High ocean freight costs to the US further inflate prices.
Extensive rely on bunker fuel for shipping. Production costs are sensitive to imported petrochemicals used in resins and adhesives.
Infrastructure limitations make regional shipping expensive; any global fuel spike causes a disproportionate rise in logistics costs.
High exposure to maritime fuel costs. Domestic energy subsidies might mitigate some production costs, but export logistics remain high-risk.
Reliance on truck-to-port logistics for balsa/tropical wood makes them vulnerable to diesel hikes. Pan-American shipping rates would increase.
Strong manufacturing base is vulnerable to electricity and gas price increases. Ocean-bound freight to the US is the primary cost driver.
Extremely sensitive to natural gas prices. Conflict strains energy markets, causing kiln drying and pressing costs to soar. Trans-Atlantic logistics costs also rise.
High-latitude harvesting is energy-intensive. Industrial wood processing in the Nordics is heavily impacted by the European energy market volatility.
Faces a 'Sanction & War Premium'. Logistics are inefficient and require high-cost workarounds. Domestic fuel for remote harvesting is hit by military requisitioning.
Intensive pine plantation harvesting relies on diesel. Long-distance shipping to North America is the largest variable in their pricing.
Like Finland, Swedish mills are sensitive to the high cost of electricity and gas required for high-grade structural plywood production.
As a major processing hub, Belgium's energy costs for secondary manufacturing and logistics through North Sea ports are high-impact areas.
Infrastructure relies heavily on diesel for harvesting and internal transport. Limited direct routes to the US mean high exposure to global bunker fuel and fees.
A re-exporter facing a 'double fuel hit': importing raw logs/veneer at higher costs, then exporting finished plywood with massive trans-Pacific surcharges.
Strong furniture and plywood industry is deeply integrated into the European gas grid, making production costs highly volatile during war.
Regional transport costs for tropical hardwoods are hit by diesel hikes. Shipping through the Caribbean/Gulf sees increased insurance premiums.
High-end decorative plywood production is energy-intensive. Italy's reliance on imported gas for industry leads to significant production cost spikes.
Industrial power costs are a major factor. The German wood-working sector faces high overheads when global oil and gas prices surge.
Benefits from land-logistics like Canada. However, manufacturing energy and petroleum-based chemicals still drive a noticeable price increase.
Heavily focused on birch plywood. Harvesting in difficult terrain and energy for processing make them highly susceptible to fuel and gas price shifts.
Being landlocked, Paraguay faces double logistics costs: river/road transport to coastal ports plus international ocean freight, both hit by fuel prices.
